comments.title: Gods are good so pray for anything and its probably fine
comments.content:
I, an adult, have an older relative who has decided they know best. This relative has turned to prayer to pray the things they've decided I need into my life to my detriment. An example: they have been bothering me about moving closer to home. Out of nowhere, I get a text with the underlying assumption that I am in some kind of trouble along with a prayer they and their prayer group have been suddenly praying for me revolving around being "on fire" for what is "good." Three days later, I have the only kitchen fire I've had in the better part of two decades I've been living on my own and had I not controlled it I would have had been in real trouble.

Complicating matters is that the relative has a hard time separating their faith and their ulterior motives, insists their faith is unfailingly good so if something happens it must be for the best, and has the low key secular attitude of its not really real, the important part is feeling like they're sending "good" vibes, and nothing could possibly be actually going on especially any bad things they certainly aren't responsible for. They're also not particularly well disposed to the idea that prayer is magic with underlying physics and therefore ethics. So its a difficult situation to address on an interpersonal level.

I was wondering what someone comfortable with Golden Dawn practice and natural magic could do to address the immediate concern of the unwelcome spiritual imposition so as to get some breathing room to sort out the underlying situation.

replies.title: Re: Gods are good so pray for anything and its probably fine
replies.content:
Yeah, there's a lot of seriously evil magic being done by people who think that they're on the side of good and can therefore pray for whatever they want, no matter how grubby the motive. I've dealt with that more than once. Here's what worked for me.

1) Keep up your daily practices: in GD terms, LBRP and Middle Pillar exercise, daily meditation, and daily divination.

2) Invoke protective powers. Look at your natal horoscope and see which of the following planets is strongest there: Sun, Mars, or Saturn. (Talk to an astrologer if you have doubts.) Look up the archangel ruling the strongest planet. Invoke that archangel, using standard GD ritual methods, and ask him for his help defending you against evil prayers. If you prefer to work with gods, invoke them instead. Do this weekly on the appropriate day.

3) Natural magic is very strong in this context. The red bag amulet and the hoodoo bath included in the Magic Monday FAQ will help considerably. You might also get a bottle of Florida Water -- a scent much used in hoodoo for its protective powers -- and splash a little on the four corners of your home.

4) Consider making a talisman of protection -- again, the strongest of the three planets listed above, in terms of your horoscope, is the one to use. Standard GD talisman rituals make good robust talismans, and those will help quite a bit.

5) If you know which god the person is invoking, talk to that god in prayer. Let him know that you're not happy about what the person is doing. Go into detail about why it's wrong and hurtful. Ask the god to deny the prayers. This can work remarkably well.

replies.title: Re: Gods are good so pray for anything and its probably fine
replies.content:
(Not OP)

Would the Florida water be splashed on the outside or inside of the home?

replies.title: Re: Gods are good so pray for anything and its probably fine
replies.content: You can do either. I tend to put it on the outside if I own the place and on the inside if I rent.

replies.title: Re: Gods are good so pray for anything and its probably fine
replies.content:
Not the OP, but I'm dealing with a similar situation.

1) How important is meditation in the short term? I'm currently in unstable living situation and finding it hard to make the time and space for it. I've taken to doing the LBRP, Middle Pillar, and R+C rituals in the bathrooms; but as I am bouncing around shared accommodations, finding time and space for meditation is tricky.

I know once I get my own place, or even just a slightly more stable arrangement, then I will be resuming meditation, but for now it's pretty hard to figure out where or when to do it...

replies.title: Re: Gods are good so pray for anything and its probably fine
replies.content: It's far and away the most important thing you can practice. Most people who fail at ceremonial magic fail because they don't practice meditation. This is true even in the short term. If you really want to, you can put five minutes a day into meditation -- yes, a bathroom is an option. Do it. You'll thank yourself later.

replies.title: Not the OP
replies.content: Can you illuminate or describe what it is that meditation does for the aspirant that ritual and divination cannot do? I imagine that it must be something to do with the development of the self and perhaps of a mental body, but can hardly form an idea of how it affects that.

replies.title: Re: Not the OP
replies.content: It's the one effective way to awaken to the fact that you are not your personality. That's crucial, because your personality aka lower self belongs to the astral plane, and is yanked around by every current in the churning astral sea -- feelings, passions, fantasies, thoughts, advertising campaigns, fads, extraordinary popular delusions and the madness of crowds, you name it. Through meditation you rise above the churn, free yourself from its power, and gain the ability to think your own thoughts, feel your own feelings, and make your own choices, most often for the first time in this and many other lives. Rituals won't do that, neither will divination, and neither will anything else -- meditation's the only known way.

replies.title: Re: Not the OP
replies.content: Is there any value in the mind emptying meditations or is it only in the discursive kind?

replies.title: Re: Not the OP
replies.content: There are various forms of meditation that are useful, but the mind-emptying meditations have to be combined with systematic mental exercise (say, studying a set of scriptures under the guidance of an experienced teacher -- this is what's done in Asian monasteries) or they can cause a lot of harm.

replies.title: Still Not the OP
replies.content:
"It's the one effective way to awaken to the fact that you are not your personality."

Some make that claim for psychedelics, but the few I've tried didn't do it and I don't feel up to any more mind-addling experiments.

"...your personality aka lower self belongs to the astral plane, and is yanked around by every current in the churning astral sea -- feelings, passions, fantasies, thoughts, advertising campaigns, fads, extraordinary popular delusions and the madness of crowds..."

So that's why life is such a confusing struggle! I'm an astral salmon swimming upstream to what I hope will be the spawning grounds, but might turn out to be a hungry grizzly bear!

Your explanation is very illuminating. I thought I was a busy Hermetic bee, but am actually a spiritual goof-off, a grasshopper forsooth.

I recently started on some simple mantra meditations. For example:

Exhale: THAT I AM

I hope that will suffice for starters.

replies.content: That's certainly the traditional teaching.

replies.title: Re: Gods are good so pray for anything and its probably fine
replies.content:
OP Here

Thank you for your advice. One follow up question: why is natural magic strong in a context where the attacker is invoking divinity? Isn't natural magic operational mostly on the etheric or astral plane while the unwanted divine attention comes from higher up?

replies.title: Re: Gods are good so pray for anything and its probably fine
replies.content: Natural magic is strong because the attacker isn't actually invoking divinity; a false image of the divine simply gives him a focus for the grubby emotions that he's pushing at you. Natural magic is always strong against nasty magic because the lower astral, which is where nasty magic operates, is just above the boundaries of the etheric and generally has to pass through the etheric plane to get to you.

comments.title: Learning Ritual Magic
comments.content:
Good Evening JMG,
Thank you for being here tonight. Hope the night will cure you of your tiredness. I recently finished ploughing through Learning Ritual Magic and completed A Ritual of Self-Initiation. The additional reading from your list of books was The Mystical Qabalah. My plan is to continue learning ceremonial magic. I'm pondering my options. Would you say working through Revisioning The Tree of Life while adapting the work to the version of the tree I'm familiar with is a good idea? Should I just follow the sequence outlined in FAQ and move to Paths of Wisdom? Any other wizardly advice?

replies.title: Re: Learning Ritual Magic
replies.content:
First of all, congratulations! That's a step most people who start that book never reach.

Second, I recommend going straight on to Paths of Wisdom and Circles of Power -- the two are meant to be done in parallel, with the first providing fodder for your daily meditations and the second teaching you the next stages of your ritual work. Those will give you a few years of work and teach you plenty about ceremonial magic, and then you can start on Regardie's The Golden Dawn for your graduate course.

replies.title: Re: Learning Ritual Magic
replies.content: Thanks for this. I had thought Paths of Wisdom and Circles of Power needed to be done in succession, so I have been dutifully going through Paths of Wisdom, trying to supplement what I have been doing with random readings of related work on the Tree of Life or Tarot. I always felt that something was missing and that I was floundering a bit, so this clarification helps tremendously. I have been reluctant to open Circles of Power until I had finished the work of Paths of Wisdom, but now I know I can start on it.

replies.title: Re: Learning Ritual Magic
replies.content: Yes, they were designed to be studied and practiced in tandem. Have fun!

comments.title: Ethnicity and Magic
comments.content:
Hi Archdruid John, and all esteemed members of the community.

I am interested in discussing how ethnicity, cultural, religious, and educational backgrounds influence a person's esoteric and magical path. How does it affect the connection to the egregore? I am especially interested in whether any of you choose to work with traditions from a different ethnic, cultural and religious tradition.

Does anyone here have experience or knowledge of Islamic magic (besides the Picatrix). Islam is dead set against magic, yet many do practice folk magic. In Malaysia we have "bomohs" who are part shaman, part witch doctor, whose activities predate the adoption of Islam. Once Islam became established (took hundreds of years), these bomohs adopted an Islamic gloss but the underlying work is animist.

Felix

replies.title: Re: Ethnicity and Magic
replies.content:
1) My direct experience is that the gods and the powers of magic literally couldn't care less about your background -- that's purely a human fixation. Most of the world's Christians, for example, aren't descended from the people who lived in the eastern Mediterranean in the first century CE, nor are most Buddhists from northern India! I have known people of Chinese and African descent who practiced Druidry and got good results from it, and I have practiced traditional Southern hoodoo, poured ale as an offering to Thor, and participated in Shinto services, all with good results, even though I don't belong to any of the ethnicities that originated these practices.

2) There I can't help you. The Picatrix is my only serious exposure to that, and I work with the Latin version from medieval Europe, not the Arabic version (because I don't read Arabic).

comments.title: Objective Clairvoyance
comments.content:
To David P.

You had asked a couple weeks ago about you experiencing reading something, for it not be there later. They way you described it, appearing in the corner of your eye and all is how I have experienced this too. One of those times it was readin g The Secret Doctrine and veered me into something very important that is still part of my life, though not all were like that. In my experience stuff like that is divinatory in nature, or rather a form of clairvoyance. It also seems to be the beginnings of what is called objective clairvoyance, I think, which means that the inner vision can overlap with the material vision as if you were seeing, what you are seeing with the inner senses, with your own eyes open and all.

replies.title: Re: Objective Clairvoyance
replies.content:
How very interesting, thank you! That would match up with the one example I can remember off the top of my head (the one I gave in that comment), where I'm reasonably certain the intent behind the text shone through.

I'm relieved to hear that I'm not the only one. This and some other visual phenomena had me worried I might be going mental. On the other hand, the "beginnings" part sure sounds like I should expect more of this, which I'm not necessarily pleased with—I quite like being able to trust my material vision, thankyouverymuch.

Curiously enough, I need to close my eyes to receive visual answers to prayers. I wonder if that's because there's not much in the material world those visuals could form around, compared to text appearing in "letter soup" in the corner of my eyes? Or, I suppose, it could simply be a matter of not having much practice with prayer, especially when it comes to listening for a response.

—David P.

replies.title: Re: Objective Clairvoyance
replies.content:
No, not mental, you are going astral ;-)

I am a relative beginner on this too but I would say: Its not a matter to worry about at all but a matter of discipline of your inner senses. If stuff starts to happen in a manner that makes you uncomfortable, very firmly will your senses shut, pinch your skin or put an ice cube in your nape if you have to (physical pain closes you down quick). All this with the held intention of not opening unless you let it happen. Talk to your subconscious in a firm but loving manner. If it's trying to tell you something, make arrangements to, say, if you want to tell me something important communicate this way or grab my attention this other way; if its an emergency, another way. If it is an opening of the senses (and it can be just a one off thing), it is not a curse but a great boon and can be put to use and to the service of others. The same way you train your subconscious mind to pull a specific tarot card for a specific meaning, you can do it with other things outside the divining table or ritual room. To me this just means our practices could be building enough pressure that they are coming through, so its just a matter of how to fit a wider world into a material existence.

So be afraid not, its a lot of fun, and awe. But I do recommend building a close relationship with deities, they will watch your back, and teach you. In the same way you listen in prayer, listen to the world, deities can speak through the wider consciousness too, not just the personal. It also never happens in my experience, in situations where you would be at risk. Say, while driving. Its attention that makes these things work, focus on material things like your weight or a piece of sculpting clay and your attention will be material; follow feelings, images and ideas and it becomes subtler and subtler; be firmly here and also there and you become a walker between worlds. Its attention because its there already, its just a matter of where you are looking at any given point of time. Deities that can help with this are Earth and Martial deities, such as Sandalphon Archangel and Mars, for stability and discipline, and messenger deities for learning the tricks, such as Anubis, or Hermes. If it ever happens, and again its not something the be afraid about, where in your meditation it seems that something is pulling you fast, or feels like falling, make the decision right there quickly, either come back to your body awareness and close down, or follow it, the in-between can be unpleasant.

Another thing, be careful with drugs and alcohol if you use them at all. Once the inner senses are trained even weed or too much alcohol can open you up, and with chemicals there is no way to control things but deep breathing to try to calm down and metabolize things faster.

As for prayer, I don't know really, might also be a question of habit. Once after prayer inside an open temple Jesus was in front and above me, not quite physically there, but my eyes were wide open, with the mental picture overlapped (not on top) with the background, tears flowing. I would say embrace it with discipline. Cool stuff!

replies.title: Re: Objective Clairvoyance
replies.content:
Thank you, this is very helpful and, once again, reassuring.

—David P.

replies.title: Re: Objective Clairvoyance

comments.content:
How do you tell if a past life has started surfacing? I recently read a Japanese short story, Grave of the Fireflies by Nosaka Akiyuki, set during and after the bombings of Kobe in World War II. The whole thing felt far too real, more so than fiction normally does for me. It almost felt like I could remember the sound the planes made overhead, the fear of being burned alive, the desperate horrible feeling of starvation, and other details of the story.

It would make sense of some things about my life: I've long had an aversion to anything related to World War II, especially the Pacific Theater; I've always felt that only idiots think there's anything good about war in general; I have a profound and inexplicable fondness for traditional Japan; and quite a few of my personality quirks are (or were) normal behavior in Japan.

I highly doubt I'm Nosaka for a large number of reasons; but one of the reasons that the story caught on is that a lot of Japanese had similar experiences during World War II to what he based the story on. I also have no idea why I felt the moment I heard of the story I needed to read it: normally my reaction to anything related to World War II, especially the Pacific Theater, is to avoid it.

Is it possible that my reaction to the short story is a past life starting to surface?

If so, do you have advice for how to integrate this lifetime? I have no idea what the rest of the life looked like, but right now, the only thing surfacing is profoundly unpleasant....

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It's hard to tell, but this sounds at least possible. I'd encourage you, despite the unpleasantness, to write down everything you remember. Then set the whole thing aside. If it's a real memory, more bits will surface over time.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: My experience of past-life memories, for what it's worth, is that they surfaced naturally -- thoughts, images, words, feelings etc just popping into my head. A key point is that I didn't make any effort to come up with these things, they came to me already formed, so it's not confabulation. It's like remembering being a different person in past times & places, yet the "exotic" settings should also feel quite familiar. They also include experiences I've never had, and knowledge beyond my grasp, which further confirms that they're genuine; no amount of confabulation could fill in blanks like that.

comments.content:
This is a follow-up to your post from last week:

https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/343321.html?thread=57410585#cmt57410585

You mentioned that brain-computer interfaces may attract demons. Could it be possible to ward and protect digital systems against such interference? Perhaps with the help of divine powers one has a relationship with? Also, are there ways for a programmer who's aware of the spiritual to place such protections on a piece of software in a way that's unobtrusive to the general user audience? Thanks.

And then my two cents on whole-brain emulation. It's not remotely possible with today's technology, and on top of that nobody has any clear idea of how consciousness actually manifests in the brain. Strokes and injuries have shed some light on how the nervous system works, but the nature of consciousness is a mystery and no attempt to implement consciousness has remotely succeeded.

It's helpful to understand how a computer is different from a brain. A computer can execute instructions extremely quickly (the gigahertz number associated with your CPU tells you how fast), but each instruction can only act on a few pieces of data at a time. So the amount of data that's changeable at any given moment is tiny. The data a computer is processing must all flow through a narrow bottleneck.

Meanwhile in the brain, everything is mutable and all parts talk to each other constantly. The behavior of neurons is so complex that each one may as well be considered a computer in its own right. These cells are constantly exchanging amounts of data through their electric signals that would slow any computer network to a halt if you tried to digitally emulate neural activity.

Emulating the brain would require dozens of phenomenal breakthroughs in computer hardware, software and psychology. Anyone claiming they can do it now is probably just selling LLM snake oil.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
> The data a computer is processing must all flow through a narrow bottleneck.

This is true in the von Neumann CPU paradigm (which is still the basic paradigm of modern computers -- though in recent decades it has been bent a little, e.g. you can get consumer-level devices with 4 "real" processing cores and another 4 "virtual" cores that act using those parts of the corresponding first 4 cores which aren't busy at any given time, on an as-available basis). "CPU" literally stands for "central processing unit". But the paradigms that GPUs usually operate in, while related, are different enough to start making up some of the distance -- some combination of single-instruction-multiple-data and many-simple-cores.

(And, of course, as for going the other direction along the edge of the engineering-feasibility envelope relating total processing to speed of local sequential operations -- as long as you aren't bottlenecked by communication among processing units or memory storage units, you can always convert a factor-of-N increase in local sequential speed directly into a factor-of-N increase in parallelism with the original slower sequential speed.)

> These cells are constantly exchanging amounts of data through their electric signals that would slow any computer network to a halt if you tried to digitally emulate neural activity.

I admit to not knowing a ton about the details of anything below the scale of "entire synapses and neuron chromosomes". As for communications on larger scales, though, the main scenario to worry about is the one where the cells' interconnections don't use classical-physics-style electrical communication, but instead do something that we achieve only with great difficulty in a quantum computer, by creating some kind of distributed quantum-state subspace that's physically protected from decoherence. (That's still a sort-of-live hypothesis!) Absent that complication, I think the cost of tracking electrical signals between one side of a boundary of a given brain volume and the other side is mostly just proportional to the number of axons crossing the boundary, times the bandwidth of the axons (maybe 1000Hz if it's a fast neuron or you don't remove much redundancy). All the other electrical signal variations can be analyzed into wavelet-like diffuse structures of various sizes in space-time, where only the largest-scale variations have effects any significant distance away from the boundary. There will only be so many wavelets of that size that can fit on the boundary (without being redundant in their description of the electrical variations), so those variations, once they are analyzed in that way, are negligibly costly to transfer information about, compared to the axons. And presumably you'd partition the emulation across GPU-like cores in more or less the natural way, with network communication being cheaper between cores that are closer together in real space, tasked with emulating brain regions that were closer together in simulated space.

Supposedly the most connection-dense part of the brain has about 200 million axons in total, in one place or another. From this we can get a rough scale of the amount of total medium-range communication you'd need in the hardware at around 200 billion axon electrical state updates per second, of which most would be passing just from one processing unit to an adjacent unit. By itself this doesn't sound prohibitive at all.

If you want to construct a counterargument to feasibility, you should be focusing either on the internal details of neurons or small regions of a few dozen neurons, or on somehow establishing the hypothesis that there was meaningful quantum computation happening. (And, if you're going to cite Penrose's arguments from metamathematical paradoxes in relation to quantum minds, first be sure to read about the form of paradox-resistance you get in a more recent conception of automating mathematics that's slightly more appropriate to living in a world of finite temporal mortals, e.g. as in https://intelligence.org/files/LogicalInductionAbridged.pdf#theorem.4.3.2 , than the default conception of metamathematics used by Penrose.)

(The last I know of more general calculations of computational requirements is from 2008: https://ora.ox.ac.uk/objects/uuid:a6880196-34c7-47a0-80f1-74d32ab98788/files/s5m60qt58t#page=79 )

> Emulating the brain would require dozens of phenomenal breakthroughs in computer hardware, software and psychology.

The idea of what it means to have sober, realistic, conservative judgement in this context is, unfortunately, subject to a kind of underdetermination in this case, in that it fractures into multiple conflicting perspectives, depending on which of the different opposed kinds of consequences one first imagines that virtue would consist in making oneself or one's audience safe against. So, if one just (however unconsciously) resolves, "I'm going to be virtuously sober, realistic, and conservative about this," then that is like engaging in an invocation ritual where the specification of what form of consciousness one is summoning is perhaps inappropriately open-ended.

(Admittedly, almost everything humanly specifiable is open-ended to some degree, possibly even including most of mathematics. But this is a matter of degree.)

If one first imagines that virtue consists in helping make oneself, one's audience, and ultimately the public safe against hype and fear and snake oil (not to mention, making oneself safe against being judged badly for being "obviously" credulous and complicit), then "sober, realistic, conservative judgement" looks like motivatedly finding reasons why a novelty would be difficult. But if one instead first imagines that virtue consists in helping make oneself, one's audience, and ultimately the public safe against the consequences if a novelty were to be made real, then "sober, realistic, conservative judgement" instead looks like searching for the paths that an attacker motivated by wealth, political power, military power, curiosity, or hubris might follow to realize the novelty, and finding ways to "responsibly" guard against those paths or their consequences.

Think of the example of the hotel safe heist, where the attacker rented a room with a wall in common with the manager's office, simply cut straight through the soft drywall, and stole the entire safe during the night. This attack bypassed all the other security difficulties that might have been brought to mind by someone who was unconsciously approaching the hotel safe security problem in terms dictated essentially by the psychology of social impression-management about responsibility, such as putting safeguards on the manager's personal character and the manager's office doors and windows and the safe combination and the keys. For the hotel owner to have gotten the right answer here would have instead required them to think through all the ways the attacker might have reconceptualized the problem of how to reach their goal.

I read the technical goal of brain emulation as possibly only requiring:

- One or two dozen small breakthroughs in figuring out how to gather data about live neurons and preserved developed brain tissues.

- One medium-to-phenomenal breakthrough in figuring out how to employ the empirically abundantly-observed Bitter Lesson phenomenon ( http://www.incompleteideas.net/IncIdeas/BitterLesson.html ) of how massive computation and data can be substituted for actual insight, in such a way as to turn coarse-grained external multi-channel observations of single neurons over time into finer-grained models of neuron-internal-states-over-time. (I can see how I would begin to do this, and I am bewildered and confusedly slightly grateful that almost everyone else doesn't seem to quite be oriented to the problem the same way.)

- One to three medium-to-phenomenal breakthroughs in figuring out how to track the development of bits of basically-matured brain tissue containing a few neurons or a few dozen neurons, to see how the dendrites and synapses and supporting cells form and dissipate and change their longer-term states and connections over time. This may be another place where the Bitter Lesson phenomenon would be applicable, but it's somewhat trickier than the case of a notionally geometrically-static neuron, since there are more categories of things to keep track of.

- One to three medium-to-phenomenal breakthroughs in figuring out how to fake or plagiarize major aspects of large-scale hypothetical brain development using data from matured brains, or in figuring out how to pass stably to some other paradigm for simulated brain organization where biological and psychological developmental plausibility are less relevant.

There's a sort of metaphorical economic input-output curve, where the input is something like "amount of insights/computing power used", and the output is "rate at which the resulting emulated brain's behavior deviates from the behavior of real brains, for the purpose of this-or-that kind of efficacy". Be careful of equivocating together different points on the output of the curve, and then deriving false beliefs about the points on the input! The number of breakthroughs and amount of computation that would be required to get up close to some asymptote of perfection, where an emulation matches the probability distribution of a brain's behavior with only some tiny epsilon nats of probability-deviation relative entropy per second, would be very high. (By way of analogy, as far as I know we still don't know how to compute the boiling point of water, just from first-principles descriptions of the particles involved, to closer than maybe a hundredth of a degree, because the self-interactions of water are so complex.) But the number of breakthroughs and amount of computation required to make a brain emulation that could become very disruptive to the existing order of life on Earth might not be very far from what is presently available at all. (To complete the analogy, we nonetheless have quite good practical simulation models of the properties of novel aqueous solutions, so long as we allow ourselves to curve-fit the models' final predictions on known states of water and known aqueous solutions. Some moderately-brute-force neural simulations trained on the 3D patterns found in undersaturated salt water solutions have even extrapolated past their data to qualitatively predict the formation of salt crystals in supersaturated salt water solutions.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
(continuing)

> Could it be possible to ward and protect digital systems against such interference?

I want to register that I wasn't trying to minimize the importance of this part of your comment by responding at such length to the other part. I just don't know enough to say much that's useful about it.

One thing I do know of, as potentially worth mentioning, has to do with a line of research on mitigations against the hypothetical "sleeper agent" attack. In this attack, the attacker prepares and publishes a digital-arithmetic alchemical tincture from digital media, but in the process infuses some secret stimulus-response patterns into the tincture, and then the victim includes the tincture for purposes of automated projections in some larger externally-exposed system. At this point the attacker can expose the tincture to special stimuli that the victim didn't know about, and the resulting projections help the attacker gain access to other parts of the victim's system that they weren't expecting.

One of the mitigations is for the victim to simply prepare a new tincture using projections from the existing published tincture, leaving out any stimuli that seem inapposite to the problem the victim had been hoping to solve. It wasn't clear in advance that this would work, but apparently it does, at least using ordinary extraction methods that aren't making use of unreasonably fine-grained fiddly details of the projections from the attacker's tincture.

(There's also a reverse result, which is that if the attacker and victim are both starting from the same original tincture, then the attacker can provide projections from a tincture they've modified by a minor further extraction, and even though those projections look innocent, if the victim makes a similar further extraction from those projections, then their resulting tincture is compromised.)

Correspondingly, then, a digital system might make for less of a conduit to malefic influences if materials more closely associated with those influences are clearly excluded from the source materials used in the last round of automation involved in producing the system.

comments.title: Help understanding a phenomenon
comments.content:
Hello. I would like to ask for help in understanding a phenomenon that happened to me yesterday. I don't practice magic nor do I intend to for the time being (having realized the path takes more dedication than I'm capable of giving it now), so this is a question that comes from a layperson. As a note of preface, there's this thing that happens with me in which when I look at certain people's pictures I get strong feelings of, for lack of better vocabulary, of their "vibe" being off. I hesitate to use the word aura since it's something more on the domain of a "physiological" reaction, a sort of serious discomfort; also, I have a big pot full of salt and bent nails in my room, where this happened.

With that being said, I was talking with a friend yesterday and she wanted to share with me some person whose research on past lives she was liking a lot. I have learned my lesson from acting like a total idiot in the past and suffering the consequences, so I don't go looking around for such things at all, but it didn't seem like it would be a big deal to just take a brief look at this researcher's website. When I did so, there were some pictures of people in séances and I got some major bad vibes and quickly scurried off; having told so to my friend, she wanted me to take a look at a photo of this researcher to see if I had the same reaction; I didn't really want to do so, but it didn't seem like nothing too bad could happen and, though I had a split-second instinctive reaction that I really oughta not do that, I did so anyway (obviously an idiotic decision on my part). What happened was the worst case of "bad vibes" I've ever had; my skin crawled, I started shivering and after a little while I was having trouble thinking coherent sentences. And though usually these things pass quickly, this time it started getting worse and worse.

In a moment of lucidity I managed to quickly look up an online version of the LBRP that I liked, having been out of practice and needing a reminder; I executed (very incompetently) the ritual and almost as soon as I started the bad vibes went away. With all this having been said, I'd like to ask for some advice or knowledge from smarter and more knowledgeable people as to what exactly might have happened, if it was of a "metaphysical" nature at all and how should I go about with this problem with pictures that at times is really inconvenient (since it happens with some famous people's pictures and sometimes their pictures just show up in variable contexts on the internet).

replies.title: Re: Help understanding a phenomenon
replies.content:
You're psychically sensitive, and specifically have a talent for psychometry -- the psychic gift of being able to read "vibes" from objects and images. That's a very mixed blessing, as you know well! I can certainly understand that you don't want to take up serious magical practice -- and that's probably wise, as your sensitivity would be a liability in that work -- but I'm going to recommend that you learn the LBRP and practice it enough that you can do it without too much fumbling. No, you don't have to do it daily, but you should have it handy for clearing away very nasty vibes. Those are unpleasantly common these days.

Oh, and don't ignore your intuition! (But then you know that now...)

replies.title: Re: Bindings and Ethics
replies.content:
Here's another story about bindings. Not a question, just my two cents' worth.

My ex-wife used to be Wiccan. She always said she believed in the Law of Threefold Return and "an it harm none." But after the attack on the Twin Towers in 2001, she decided to Do Her Part by putting a binding on Osama bin Laden so that he couldn't do anything. (I think she might have targeted Saddam Hussein as well.) I specifically remember that she made a doll to represent Osama, tied it up with twine (so that it "couldn't do anything"), and threw it in the back of the closet. comments.title: Precision and ambiguity in words
comments.content:
Dear JMG

When doing a magical working, how far does one need to take the instruction to be precise and unambiguous in wording?

For example if someone wants to be more attractive, and has meditated on this goal and figured out how it relates to the ritual elements, is it necessary to specify in words "romantically/sexually attractive" rather than just "attractive" to avoid the technical ambiguity that "I am attractive" could mean "attractive as an employee" or "attractive to blood sucking insects"? I.E if the person is internally sure what they mean and feel the simple description expresses it, is that enough?

Thanks

replies.title: Re: Precision and ambiguity in words
replies.content: It depends on the working. If you're doing something with your own power, or working with gods and goddesses, as long as you know exactly what you mean and put that feeling into the words you use, you're fine. If you're working with other spirits, and especially with evil spirits, you have to be absolutely precise, because most spirits will take your words absolutely literally, and evil spirits will go out of your way to twist your words to your downfall. (This is one of the many reasons why trying to mooch favors from spirits is a bad idea.)

comments.title: Navigating the Current Spectacle - A Magical Approach?
comments.content:
Navigating the Current Spectacle

Upthread:
"Can you illuminate or describe what it is that meditation does for the aspirant that ritual and divination cannot do? I imagine that it must be something to do with the development of the self and perhaps of a mental body, but can hardly form an idea of how it affects that.

And your response:

"It's the one effective way to awaken to the fact that you are not your personality. That's crucial, because your personality aka lower self belongs to the astral plane, and is yanked around by every current in the churning astral sea -- feelings, passions, fantasies, thoughts, advertising campaigns, fads, extraordinary popular delusions and the madness of crowds, you name it. Through meditation you rise above the churn, free yourself from its power, and gain the ability to think your own thoughts, feel your own feelings, and make your own choices, most often for the first time in this and many other lives. Rituals won't do that, neither will divination, and neither will anything else -- meditation's the only known way."
https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/344283.html?thread=57483739#cmt57483739 :

By meditation I assume you are referring to Discursive Meditation - not the empty mind of the idiot! Where we use meditation to often be a method to visualise working with the body's energy system (think acupuncture channels and the like) - creating body and mind states that seem useful, discursive meditation is about exploring ideas and thoughts yet also develops the mental sheath.

This has got me thinking about psychic/psychological self defence as an active (and yet passive!) state of awareness and action rather than simple prescription of technique. 'Psychic self defence' is not quite apt, but neither is 'Psychological self defence' - it is more subtle than that because of the balance needed between the 'outer' spectacle, one's 'place' in that. and also the requirement to raise oneself not only beyond one's own lower tendencies but also the need to rise above the polarities of the spectacle, self and wider world.

At the moment I've added 'The Spectacle' as a metaphor to consider in this, but if you were to set themes for meditation, what basic, intermediate and advanced themes might you suggest? Or shall I go and meditate on that? ;)

And having done so, doh! of course, there is not a one size fits all answer to this as our paths are all different!
I'll rephrase the question:
If you were to suggest three generic themes for this meditative purpose (basic, intermediate, advanced) what might they look like?

EW

replies.title: Re: Navigating the Current Spectacle - A Magical Approach?
replies.content:
1) Yes, that's a fine theme for meditation.

2) If I had to develop a course of meditations on the Spectacle, I'd take a year or so to work on that in my own morning meditations and work out a set of themes that did what needs to be done. Since I don't have a year to spend on that before the end of this Magic Monday, you know, I'll have to pass.

3) See above. I don't recommend generic themes at all -- the more specific a theme is, the better. You might consider getting a copy of Debord's Society of the Spectacle, read through it once, then go back over it and note every sentence that sounds like a good theme. Write out the sentences by hand in a notebook, and then use those for your themes for daily meditation for the next year or two.

replies.title: Re: Navigating the Current Spectacle - A Magical Approach?
replies.content:
Sorry I was not clear; the spectacle as metaphor is merely to bring another variable in to consider alongside my existing practices rather than a core area to study (at least at this point). By generic, I'm thinking of the processes of meditation rather than the specific questions like the ones you mentioned:

whether it is
what it is
how it is
who (or whose) it is
where it is
when it is
why it is
whether it becomes
what it becomes...(and so on.)
That gives you 49 inquiries. When you reach a standstill with a theme for meditation, break out the conceptions and questions, and combine the inquiries with the theme.

Mainly it occurred to me that I don't know how others approach finding and exploring themes but you saying: "read through it once, then go back over it and note every sentence that sounds like a good theme." is similar to the process I use and me saying: "If you were to suggest three generic themes for this meditative purpose (basic, intermediate, advanced) what might they look like?" would have been better said as:

Have you found that meditation at different levels of experience employ different techniques of practice or is the fundamental approach the same and more a matter of 'depth'?

Basically I was more interested in how you might approach the process at different levels of experience.
e.g. basic try this method, intermediate try that and this, advanced try that, this and the other.

replies.title: Re: Navigating the Current Spectacle - A Magical Approach?
replies.content: In my experience the techniques of practice vary quite widely, but it's not a matter of levels of experience. It requires different approaches to extract meaning from a symbolic image than from a text, and different kinds of imagery (or for that matter different kinds of text) require different approach. I have yet to see a taxonomy for this that works; it's much more a matter of learning through experience and adapting on the fly.

replies.title: Re: Navigating the Current Spectacle - A Magical Approach?
replies.content:
Thank you; a different view point is very helpful!

I haven't dwelt on much of the imagery that comes up in meditation, thinking it may or may not be useful and not wanting to get hung up on 'oh look at the shiny'.
Extracting meaning from symbolic imagery is not something I've done much of, but where thinking of things like Platonic solids, mainly try to hold images in the mind's eye and see what comes into mind. Sometimes it feels like playing with a mental Rubic cube.

replies.title: Re: Navigating the Current Spectacle - A Magical Approach?
replies.content: It's worth learning to work with imagery. Tarot cards, astrological emblems, and many other traditional diagrams are meant to be unpacked that way.

replies.content: Something I have perhaps neglected too long - would the sacred geometry oracle serve for the purpose? It is sitting on the shelf next to me!

comments.title: Mummies, the Curse of the Pharaohs, etc
comments.content:
There was an intriguing discussion recently about etheric revenants, which began in ancient times as humans becoming a guardian spirit for their tribe, a sensible magical practice; which degraded into prolonging the incarnations of god-kings, an act of hubris; finally it decayed into vampirism, a depraved remnant.
Having briefly traced out its lengthy history, I'm wondering about the pharaohs. I gather that they were fine, so long as the priestly class brought them victuals and did rites for them, but when the dynasties collapsed, they were left without a support network, and had to venture out to find life-force to feed on (including from human feces, yick).
This must be the origin of the whole mummy legend, correct? But whatever became of the pharaohs? Are they still stuck in a living death underneath the pyramids, or did they escape into reincarnation somehow? Also, does this relate to that whole thing of Egyptian tomb raiders getting cursed, or is that a separate matter?

replies.title: Re: Mummies, the Curse of the Pharaohs, etc
replies.content:
1) Once the pharaohs weren't getting fed, their etheric revenants began to break down. I've examined quite a few Egyptian mummies in museums, and there's nobody home - many centuries ago, after their offering temples were abandoned and their tombs were looted, their etheric revenants collapsed completely and they went through the Second Death and on into the cycle of reincarnation.

2) It's a different thing. The ancient Egyptians were extremely good at practical magic, and the pharaohs had priests put robust curses on anyone who disturbed their tombs.

replies.title: Re: Mummies, the Curse of the Pharaohs, etc
replies.content: I would suspect that the monks of Mount Yudono, and maybe a few hidden places in China, India, and whatever goes on in the Asian parts of Russia, might be the last places where offerings are made to revenants. Certainly from what I've read of the sokushinbutsu, the practice at the moment seems reasonably clean, doesn't seem to have descended into a vampire cult. This is probably helped by the monks themselves, there's no hint of power or grandeur, unlike the pharoahs.

replies.title: Re: Mummies, the Curse of the Pharaohs, etc
replies.content: The monks of Senninzawa have kept it fenced around with their own strict ascetic spirituality, a very effective way of chasing off the unworthy. I'd be worried if it survives in China, though, because China has a very rich cultural tradition of vampirism, pretty clearly motivated by the craving for immortality at all costs that the less reputable end of Taoism has fostered.

replies.title: Re: Mummies, the Curse of the Pharaohs, etc
replies.content:
This reminds me of comments I've read, both serious and mocking, describing Taoists using tantric practices and celibacy in an attempt at longer life. I've also read various Chinese tales of alchemists seeking an elixir of immortality, and accidentally inventing gunpowder, or some such thing. I guess this all comes from the "butt end" of those traditions, so to speak.

Also, random aside, but I wonder if Buddhist lore of "hungry ghosts" is related to etheric revenants, particularly in their debased form?

replies.title: Re: Mummies, the Curse of the Pharaohs, etc
replies.content:
There are definitely effective methods of life prolongation in the Chinese occult traditions, ranging across a gamut from spiritual attainment to sexual vampirism. As always, it's when people can't hack the spiritual attainment that they start looking for easier ways, and land in trouble.

As for hungry ghosts -- good gods. That hadn't occurred to me, but you're almost certainly correct.

replies.title: Re: Mummies, the Curse of the Pharaohs, etc
replies.content:
That makes sense, thanks.

And yeah, having studied Buddhism a fair amount, the connection with pretas suddenly lit up -- ethereal beings, in the material world yet cut off from it, craving life-energy, ppl leaving out offerings for them to consume, etc, it all lines up perfectly.
From Infogalactic, the based wiki clone I like to use:

"The term 餓鬼 èguǐ, literally "hungry ghost", is the Chinese translation of the term preta in Buddhism. "Hungry ghosts" play a role in Chinese Buddhism and Taoism as well as in Chinese folk religion. The term is not to be confused with the generic term for "ghost",鬼 guǐ (i.e. the spirit of a deceased ancestor). The understanding is that all people become such a regular ghost when they die,[1] and would then slowly weaken and eventually die a second time.[2][3] Hungry ghosts, by contrast, are a much more exceptional case..."

The fact that they're not only from Buddhism, but also Chinese folk religion, really fits with etheric revenant lore percolating into the East Asian world. Notably, the description of regular ghosts also lines up with what you said about the normal first & second death of the material and etheric bodies respectively. And a later passage distinguishes between different types of pretas, in better and worse condition, which also fits.

Thus, I've figured out the origin of mummies, vampires, and hungry ghosts: etheric revenants. Fascinating stuff iah.

replies.title: Re: Mummies, the Curse of the Pharaohs, etc
replies.content: I don't get life prolongation. It seems that you're wasting precious time trying to artificially prolong your life when you can just let your temporary bodies die and move on to the next life to further develop your mental sheath into a mental body.

replies.title: Re: Mummies, the Curse of the Pharaohs, etc
replies.content: Sure, if you've devoted at least part of your time to spiritual advancement. The problem comes when people who don't want to do that still want to dodge death -- and they often have good reason to do so, as they've racked up a lot of nasty karma.

replies.title: Re: Mummies, the Curse of the Pharaohs, etc
replies.content:

Doesn't that lead to them getting even more nasty karma? So even in their situation, it would be better for them to just die and face up to their nasty karma, instead of prolonging their life and generating more nasty karma that they have to deal with in future incarnations.

replies.title: Re: Mummies, the Curse of the Pharaohs, etc

replies.title: Re: Mummies, the Curse of the Pharaohs, etc
replies.content:
I see, thanks! I figured the curses were another matter, and despite rational-materialist naysaying, they do seem to have kept their potency to this day.

As for the Second Death, I've heard this term used in various ways, ranging from the dissolution of the soul to the Protestant Lake of Fire. I assume in this context, it means the end of one's bid to outlive material death, and rejoining the natural cycle of reincarnation. And that for most humans, the first and second deaths are in the same time-frame.

If I may ask one more: what sort of karmic consequences would there be for the pharaohs? I'd imagine they'd get into trouble for upsetting the natural order, but not to an insurmountable extent.

replies.title: Re: Mummies, the Curse of the Pharaohs, etc
replies.content:
1) In occult jargon, the second death is the death of the etheric body; the first death is the death of the material body. The second death usually happens a few days after the first.

2) Most of them won't have had any particular karmic burden, since they were just doing what was expected of them; doing your duty as best you can is a source of tolerably good karma. Those who invented the system may be in trouble, but they'll have had plenty of time to work things off.

replies.title: Re: music meditation?
replies.content: There's quite a bit of that in India and Japan, where certain kinds of traditional music are always played in a meditative state. In the West, there was one in ancient times, there were attempts to recover it in the Renaissance -- weirdly enough, that's where opera came from! -- and some modern attempts along the same lines; I haven't followed up on any of them, though.

replies.title: Re: music meditation?
replies.content: The tradition of sacred chant (e.g., Gregorian chant) in the Western church is an example of meditation using music; not sure if it's the same thing as a meditation ON music. The abandonment of that genre of music in most parishes after Vatican II may have a lot to do with the decline of the Catholic Church (although I've heard that it's making a comeback in some places). I managed to find a very traditional Catholic church a number of years ago and sang in a chant choir, and found it very powerful spiritually.

replies.title: Re: music meditation?
replies.content: In response to JMG’s answer, the composers of the late Renaissance had a theory of music, called the Doctrine of Affections. In particular, Monteverdi was a big proponent of the theory that music could be used to describe specific emotions. He was the originator of opera, I believe; and most all of his music is intended to evoke (there’s that word again) a stream of definite emotional feelings in the attentive listener.

replies.title: Re: music meditation?
replies.content:
Monteverdi was the first composer of opera to win public success, but he didn't invent it. That honor belongs to the members of the Florentine Camerata, a loose association of intellectuals in Florence in the late 16th century. That said, you're not wrong, because the doctrine of affections was widely held at the time and the members of the Florentine Camerata were all over it.

(The reason I happen to know this bit of music trivia is that it played a role in the backstory of my novel The Nyogtha Variations, in which the main character writes a chamber opera. Yes, it's also a fantasy novel featuring shoggoths.)

replies.title: Re: Surrealist magic, and demons

By reading through the posts on one of the last magic monday sessions, I caught the statement that you shouldn't work with the planetary energies of planets that are afflicted in your natal horoscope.

1. Do you count just the planets essential dignities, triplicity and aspects or also the accidental dignities, as well as terms and faces also? I find it hard to completely fit a planet in the afflicted or not afflicted corner, because most of them have some good and some bad features. My Mars for instance is in Taurus, it's fall, but because I am born at night, also in it's triplicity (as participating ruler). Mars is also in the tenth house, very close (2 degrees) to the MC. It has a square towards Venus, but also a sextil to saturn and a trigon tor mercury. Would Mars in your eyes be afflicted with these features orokay to work with?

2. Would you not use an afflicted planet in making a planetary talisman only or also not use the planet in an invoking ritual?

Grateful for your answer as alwyays. Blessed be the magic mondays......

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Okay, first of all, this is *only* valid for the sort of astrological magic that gets all its power from the planetary configurations at the time of the working. If you're using Golden Dawn-style magic and invoking divine names, it does not apply -- quite the contrary, Israel Regardie pointed out that GD-style talismans can be used for horoscope repair.

If, and only if, you're talking about medieval and Renaissance astrological magic, essential dignities and squares or oppositions with malefics are the main things that shout "No!" A planet in his detriment or fall is a definite no, irrespective of other factors.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thank you very much.

I was taking about the GD talismans and rituals. I'm getting more knowledgeable every monday, but sometimes it seems also confused by the sheer amount of different lore discussed on here.

I wish you a wonderful week.....

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
If it's GD talismans and workings, ignore your horoscope. The GD workings call on the sephiroth, not the planets as such, and so draw on powers that are above time and above the heavens, and not subject to astrological forces at all.

That said, always, but always, do divination before you attempt any form of practical magic, to make sure it's a good idea.

comments.title: Enneagram - occult influences?
comments.content:
Hi JMG! Thank you so much for all you do. In particular I am incredibly excited about the FHR, MOE, and Octagon Society material you are sharing. I'm relatively new to all this, and am very grateful for your general guidance in this space, which I trust greatly.

My question is whether the enneagram is related to numerology, sacred geometry, or has other occult influences, to your knowledge? I've found it helpful as a self inquiry tool, but perceive it's origin to be a bit mysterious. Is it a modern invention, or does it have deeper roots and/or influences? Any compatibilities / incompatibilities with other traditions you are aware of or would anticipate?

replies.title: Re: Enneagram - occult influences?
replies.content: The enneagram comes out of the work of George I. Gurdjieff, a Greek-Armenian spiritual teacher of the early 20th century. He claimed that he received it from a secret order in Central Asia called the Sarmoung Brotherhood, which nobody else has ever been able to trace. I don't know a lot about its compatibilities, having never really looked into it -- that's an end of esotericism I've never gotten around to following up on.

replies.title: Re: Enneagram - occult influences?
replies.content: Well. I was correct about it being mysterious :-) Thanks for the lead, and if I have any insight as I delve into it more, I'll be sure to share.

comments.title: Afterlife
comments.content:
Hi JMG-
In a previous post, you mentioned having unpleasant memories of your previous time between incarnations. Without prying too much, what was unpleasant about it? Is there anything that we can do to make our time between incarnations more or less pleasant?
Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Afterlife
replies.content: I don't recommend dying relatively young in a head-on car crash and being wild with grief and rage when you enter the state between lives. Your emotional and mental state at death has quite a bit of impact on what part of the inner planes you spend the first part of the time between lives, which is why the last rites of any religion are so helpful; it would have helped if I had anyone to pray for me, but I didn't. So it was pretty hellish -- a storm of bitter passions and ugly emotions resonating with the ones I already felt.

replies.title: Re: Afterlife
replies.content: I am so sorry that this happened to you!

replies.title: Re: Afterlife
replies.content: Thank you; so am I. :-(  There were karmic reasons for it, but it messed me over good and proper.

replies.title: Re: Afterlife
replies.content: Thank you for this information; do you have any books that you would recommend reading about this? I’m still relatively young, but I want to be prepared for when it does happen.

replies.title: Re: Afterlife
replies.content: Dion Fortune's book Through the Gates of Death is a useful guide; another is Manly P. Hall's From Death to Rebirth.

replies.title: Re: Afterlife
replies.content: Does this mean that anybody who die in any unpleasant way - accident, murder, in pain, etc. - are likely to have a hard time of it between lives, at least for the first part?

replies.title: Re: Afterlife
replies.content: No. You have to work fairly hard to be as stupid as I was.

replies.title: Re: Afterlife
replies.content:
So I guess you experienced the hellish lower astral, for at least a little bit? Yeesh, my condolences if so.
I remember you describing it like a pitch black nightmare, where a horrifying experience keeps happening on loop, and that's the normal state of consciousness there. Maybe you were speaking from experience?
Personally, I don't remember where I was between lives. From death by drowning when the Titanic sunk, to when my current self was born (late 20th century), it's just a blank.

replies.title: Re: Afterlife
replies.content: Yes, I was speaking from experience. Fortunately the memories are blurred and distant.

replies.title: Re: Afterlife
replies.content: Odd question but: does the pain of the memories of being hit by karma also count toward working off any lingering karma?

replies.title: Re: Afterlife
replies.content: It depends entirely on how you deal with it. Accept it and confront it, and yes, it can be a positive step. Run from it -- not so much.

replies.title: Re: Afterlife
replies.content: Once again, condolences. Likewise, my memory of dying contains the knowledge of how unpleasant it was, but it's like I'm insulated from it, seeing my own experience through a pane of glass, kind of.

replies.title: Re: Afterlife
replies.content:
Thank you. I used to revisit the experience, very lightly symbolized, in repetitive dreams -- utterly horrible pitch black nightmares where I was fighting a huge shadowy opponent, but no matter how hard I struggled I couldn't hurt him at all. Weirdly, what got me out of those dreams wasn't acceptance, or any of the other clichés. I discovered that while I couldn't hurt him, the dream-surroundings could -- and I started grabbing his arm, flinging myself to one side, and slamming him face first into various hard objects. It was utterly vivid and hideously realistic -- I know from other contexts (don't ask) what brains look like when a skull gets crushed, and that's what I experienced in the dream -- but that was the last dream of that kind I ever had.

It's probably not coincidental that I died, at the end of my last life, by going headfirst into the windshield of a car. So the death I inflicted in the dream was the death I'd suffered in life. Make of that what you will.

replies.title: (no subject)

It's different. A peregrine planet is disconnected and drifting, and the person who has that in their natal horoscope will have trouble accessing the planet's strengths for good or ill. As for detriment and fall, detriment is weak, and fall is nasty!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
This isn't much of a question, more to share:

Around a week ago on Sunday, I had an unprompted thought come to me, as an idea that hits you in the shower. It was about a person who I knew briefly 25+ years ago but have long since lost track of. The idea was about how I could remind myself of their identity after all this time. Memories have faded and this would be (an entirely legal and ethical, nothing shady) way of revisiting what I remembered of them.

The idea worked. But I don't know if it should have. Within 24 hours, I was ill with a fever that kept me in bed for the better part of the next two days. (It was running through our household, so not exactly a surprise affliction.) During that fever-haze I was thinking of the long-lost person and with a great emotional intensity. The experience dragged up a lot of old memories, and it turns out, a lot of sadness and guilt that I hadn't acknowledged before.

The one upside is that I was forced to confront a lot of old unaddressed memories and feelings around this person. I made my peace with this person and was able to acknowledge that there's nothing I could do about how things ended between us. I don't judge that I did this person any great wrong, but there was lasting guilt there and no realistic option for making amends in person.

The experience has left me rattled, even though I see it was a necessary part of healing by confronting the unaddressed feelings. I'm still not fully over whatever physical crud hit me but the release of psychic/spiritual weight is almost tangible.

Ever since I began doing the Octagon Society material, and daily SoP/divination/meditation, I'm finding more incidents like this from my past well up, looking for healing. This is one of the oldest and most powerful so far.

comments.title: Post on Values and Ethics and Question on Emotions Arising During Meditation
comments.content:
Good Evening,

I hope that the full moon in Aries isn't bringing too much craziness into anyone's lives! (My younger daughter's natal Moon is in Aries, so I'm well familiar with what a handful this combination can manifest as!)

To Share: I've kept up with my introductory series over at Rhetoric for the Renaissance Man, this time starting to dig into how to think of values and ethics, and why they're important to have, as a set up for applying them to rhetoric/persuasion (coming part 2 later this week): https://rhetoricfortherenaissanceman.com/2025/10/01/values-and-ethics-part-1/

To Ask: Occasionally when meditating, something I'm thinking through will trigger a strong emotional response, like beginning to cry. In such a case, is it better to let the emotions run their course (on the assumption that getting to that emotional reaction was precisely the right thing for the meditation to lead to), or is it better to try to return to the meditative frame of mind and keep on discoursing (on the assumption that the point of meditation is to train yourself to maintain the meditative state)? Or perhaps something else? Thus far, I've erred on the side of the former, which seems to have worked out just fine, but I was curious if there was an accepted answer.

As always, thanks very much to JMG and those who read and comment.

My blessings to all who welcome them,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Post on Values and Ethics and Question on Emotions Arising During Meditation
replies.content: It really varies from person to person. Try both and see what works best for you.

comments.content:
Sliding a question in late, which I keep meaning to ask.

Over the summer, an elderly woman I knew slightly died, of natural causes (she was quite old and frail). A day or two before she died, a few people thought they saw her in church. They said they had seen her across the room, had caught her eye, she nodded and smiled at them, but they didn't actually speak. They didn't realize how sick she was, and thought she was feeling better and had come out for the services. But she was home on hospice and near death at the time. Was this a case of mistake identity, or did she somehow "attend" a last service? I'm given to understand that only a few people "saw" her, some in the church and some outside, but that they were sure it was her even though they didn't get close enough to speak. This is all second-hand, obviously, but I'm curious if something unusual might have been going on here?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: She may very well have attended one more service. Sightings of people who have just died, visiting places or people who meant a lot to them in life, are far and away the most common form of ghost phenomenon.
